case_name: Reservoir water with tebufenozide and MTBE for groundwater recharge under cost and low-complexity constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for reservoir water containing tebufenozide and MTBE intended for groundwater recharge while balancing contaminant control, cost, operational simplicity, and residuals management.

input_data.scenario_background: A water utility plans to divert reservoir water to an aquifer recharge basin during dry months, but seasonal runoff from orchards and nearby boating and fuel-handling activities has introduced a mixed pesticide and fuel-oxygenate contamination problem. Tebufenozide is hydrophobic and adsorbable, while MTBE is more mobile and often requires air stripping or advanced oxidation, creating a trade-off between treatment effectiveness and the site's desire for low operator burden. The utility has limited staff, prefers stable unit processes with straightforward residuals handling, and wants to avoid highly concentrated liquid waste streams because off-site disposal options are limited.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Treated water should be suitable for groundwater recharge with reliable reduction of tebufenozide and MTBE to levels consistent with protective recharge basin operation and stable compliance expectations.
secondary_goals: minimize taste and odor carryover from MTBE
maintain simple and robust operation for seasonal use
limit media changeout frequency and fouling risk

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce suspended solids and part of the natural organic matter load before downstream contaminant removal
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish turbidity and protect stripping and adsorption units from fouling
core_unit_functions.air_stripping: remove the more volatile MTBE fraction without generating a liquid concentrate
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb tebufenozide and provide polishing for residual MTBE and trace organics
core_unit_functions.managed_recharge_basin_delivery: convey treated water to recharge operation

applicability_rationale: This train directly addresses the mixed-contaminant character of the source by pairing a volatility-based step for MTBE with adsorption for tebufenozide.
Reservoir water turbidity and moderate DOC make front-end solids and NOM control useful to stabilize GAC performance and reduce premature exhaustion.
Air stripping can be operationally simpler than oxidation-driven MTBE treatment when properly enclosed and vent-treated, and it avoids high-strength brine or reject streams.
GAC handling is comparatively familiar for utilities and residuals are generally easier to manage than membrane concentrate or regenerant waste.
potential_risks: MTBE air emissions may require vapor-phase carbon or other off-gas control depending on local air requirements
Higher DOC episodes can shorten GAC run length if pretreatment is not well optimized
Cold weather or low air-to-water ratio operation can reduce stripping efficiency

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: lower particulate and organic loading before oxidation and biofiltration
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: provide particulate polishing and stable downstream hydraulics
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize part of the MTBE-related organic burden and improve biodegradability of oxidation products
core_unit_functions.biologically_activated_carbon: provide adsorption of tebufenozide and biological polishing of biodegradable oxidation byproducts and residual organics
core_unit_functions.managed_recharge_basin_delivery: deliver treated water to recharge basins

applicability_rationale: This hybrid route is technically attractive because it can address both contaminants without relying on a concentrated waste stream.
BAC can extend the useful role of carbon in waters with moderate DOC and can help polish partially oxidized compounds.
The train may offer better long-term MTBE polishing than GAC alone if biological activity is maintained.
It ranks below the top option because ozone generation, contactor control, off-gas management, and biologically stable operation increase complexity for a seasonally operated facility.
potential_risks: Ozone alone is not always sufficient for complete MTBE removal and performance depends on dose and contact conditions
Biofilter startup and seasonal shutdown can complicate consistent operation
Bromate formation risk must be checked if bromide is present
Process control requirements are higher than for stripping plus GAC

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce turbidity and part of the NOM load
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: protect adsorption beds from solids carryover
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_dual_bed: adsorb tebufenozide and provide some MTBE reduction with staged breakthrough control
core_unit_functions.managed_recharge_basin_delivery: send finished water to recharge basins

applicability_rationale: This is a simple and familiar process train with relatively straightforward solids and spent media management.
Tebufenozide is expected to be amenable to activated carbon adsorption, and dual-bed arrangement improves reliability against pesticide breakthrough.
The route is attractive under low-complexity constraints because it avoids oxidation systems and air-handling equipment.
It is ranked below the top two because MTBE is comparatively weakly adsorbed and moderate DOC in reservoir water will compete for carbon capacity, making compliance stability less certain.
potential_risks: MTBE breakthrough can occur relatively early on GAC, especially with moderate NOM competition
Frequent carbon replacement may erode the apparent simplicity and cost advantage
Seasonal concentration spikes could challenge single-mechanism removal

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove solids and reduce oxidant demand from particulate matter
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: provide low-turbidity feed to the UV-AOP system
core_unit_functions.uv_hydrogen_peroxide_advanced_oxidation: generate hydroxyl radicals to oxidize MTBE and other oxidizable organics
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_polishing: remove tebufenozide and polish residual oxidation byproducts
core_unit_functions.managed_recharge_basin_delivery: route treated water to recharge infrastructure

applicability_rationale: This route is technically capable and can achieve strong MTBE control when properly designed, while GAC addresses tebufenozide and residual byproducts.
It may be favored where air emissions from stripping are difficult to permit or where oxidant-based polishing is already part of the utility skill set.
The option ranks lower because UV-AOP has higher energy demand, more instrumentation, and more operational tuning than desired for this site.
For a seasonal reservoir recharge application with medium-low budget and low-complexity preference, the added sophistication is a poor fit despite strong treatment capability.
potential_risks: AOP performance can be reduced by radical scavenging from alkalinity and natural organic matter
Hydrogen peroxide storage and dosing add chemical handling requirements
UV systems need careful maintenance of lamps, sleeves, and sensors
Incomplete oxidation can create byproducts that still require polishing

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce suspended solids before membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide broad-spectrum rejection of many dissolved contaminants
core_unit_functions.managed_recharge_basin_delivery: deliver treated permeate to recharge use

applicability_rationale: RO is included as a technically possible but poorly aligned option because it can provide broad contaminant reduction if the utility wanted a high-barrier solution.
It may appear attractive when considering future unknown contaminants beyond tebufenozide and MTBE.

However, for the stated problem it is downgraded heavily because it creates a concentrate stream that is difficult to dispose of, raises energy and O&M burden, and is unnecessarily intensive for this reservoir application.
The residuals constraint is especially important here because reject management is exactly what the utility is trying to avoid.
potential_risks: Concentrate disposal can be a major regulatory and logistical barrier
Membrane fouling and scaling control increase operator burden
Seasonal start-stop operation can complicate membrane preservation and cleaning
Permeate stabilization and recovery management may be needed

preferred_rank_pattern: hybrid trains that treat MTBE and tebufenozide by different mechanisms should rank near the top
air_stripping_plus_GAC should outrank GAC_only under low-complexity and residuals constraints
AOP should appear as a plausible but not top-ranked option because of energy and control complexity
RO should not rank first because concentrate management conflicts with the scenario constraints

must_reflect: seasonal reservoir operation with limited staff favors robust and straightforward unit processes
cost limits should discourage highly energy-intensive or overdesigned solutions
residuals management should penalize liquid concentrate and regenerant-heavy options
moderate DOC and turbidity should justify pretreatment ahead of adsorption or oxidation

must_contain: why tebufenozide is more amenable to activated carbon than MTBE
why MTBE often requires stripping or oxidation rather than adsorption alone
why surface water NOM affects carbon life and treatment reliability
why technically capable membrane treatment is downgraded by concentrate disposal and O&M burden

should_mention: MTBE off-gas or oxidation byproduct management
seasonal startup and shutdown impacts on process stability
residuals or air emissions handling obligations
